## Implications for Trading Systems [PLACEHOLDER]
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The empirical findings carry several implications for the design of deep RL
trading systems operating on limit order book data, beyond the specific
AAPL configuration studied here.

**Microstructure-aware features are a necessary, not optional, ingredient.**
The performance gap between the extended and baseline feature sets demonstrates
that order-book-derived microstructure information — order flow imbalance, spread
regime, book shape, and temporal encoding — carries signal that aggregate snapshot
quantities do not. This finding is consistent with the theoretical argument from
the microstructure literature reviewed in Chapter 2: the information relevant to
short-horizon price prediction is embedded in the dynamics of the limit order book
rather than in best-quote snapshots alone. For practitioners designing RL state
representations, this implies that the choice of which features to engineer is
at least as consequential as the choice of algorithm. A well-specified feature
set operating with a standard actor-critic architecture will likely outperform a
poorly-specified feature set using a more sophisticated model.

**Continuous-action actor-critic methods are best suited to position-sizing tasks.** The continuous action space used here allowed
the agent to express varying degrees of directional conviction through position
size, rather than being constrained to binary buy/sell decisions. This expressiveness
is important in high-frequency settings where the economically correct position
magnitude varies with the strength of the microstructure signal. Discrete-action
methods require an arbitrary grid approximation that either loses this precision
or re-introduces the curse of dimensionality. The TD3 stability mechanisms —
clipped double Q-learning, delayed policy updates, and target policy smoothing —
proved important in the noisy high-frequency reward environment: without them,
value overestimation would have driven the policy toward unreliable actions
during early training.

**Reward design should separate the training signal from the evaluation metric.**
A consistent finding across both the literature and the experiments in this
thesis is that optimizing a shaped reward — in this case the Differential Sharpe
Ratio — does not guarantee that the cumulative shaped reward will rank-correlate
with realized financial performance. These must be tracked separately. The
practical implication is that deep RL trading systems require two distinct evaluation
pipelines: a training diagnostic that tracks the optimization objective, and a
financial evaluation that rolls out the deterministic policy on held-out data and
computes return, Sharpe ratio, drawdown, and turnover from the realized position
series. Conflating these — for example, treating a high training DSR as evidence
of a strong out-of-sample Sharpe — is a common source of overoptimistic claims
in the literature.

**Execution assumptions impose a binding constraint on achievable net
performance.** The mid-price execution proxy used here represents an
upper bound on realizable returns. The spread-crossing cost that arises in live
execution is of the same order of magnitude as the short-horizon price movements
the agent attempts to capture, and the agent's demonstrated edge does not
survive at high transaction cost levels, as shown in the sensitivity analysis.
For a trading system to be considered deployable — rather than a proof of
concept — the same policy must be re-evaluated under explicit bid/ask execution
rules, realistic fee schedules, and queue-position uncertainty. The present
results establish that a useful signal exists in the microstructure-aware state
representation; they do not establish that this signal survives the full cost
structure of live high-frequency execution.
